reasoning: D and B are the two strongest: both pair the demand-side Fermi chain with an independent BLS top-down cross-check and explicitly reconcile the two methods, which is the core rigor move this problem rewards. D edges ahead because it goes furthest on bias-awareness (explicitly names and guards against anchoring on the famous textbook number, survivorship bias, and confirmation-bias salience of famous founder-engineers), adds a third cross-check (Piano Technicians Guild membership), uses an explicit Bayesian partition for (b), and lands on the metro figure (~180) that is closest to ground truth while still reporting the city figure. B is nearly as good and slightly cleaner, with an honest two-method disagreement that it shades correctly. A is solid and well-calibrated on (b) but relies on a single multiplied demand chain for (a) with no top-down cross-check, costing it rigor and depth. C is weakest: it inflates engineering to "low-to-mid 30s percent (~32%)" for (b), a factual error contradicting the survey consensus the others correctly cite (~20-25%), and it omits the BLS cross-check and city/metro dual handling, so despite a tidy table its rigor and calibration suffer.
